## Answer

**[well-established]** Every listed UFO-A construct can be represented as RDF 1.2 data, including statements about class membership and statements about those statements.  
**[well-established]** RDF 1.2, OWL 2 and ordinary N3 do not themselves assign possible-world semantics to named graphs or triple reifiers; RDF 1.2 explicitly leaves such dataset semantics to an extension. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/), [RDF 1.2 Semantics §10](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-semantics/#rdf-datasets), [N3](https://www.w3.org/TeamSubmission/n3/)

**[plausible]** All constructs are executable and decidable over a finite, explicitly enumerated Kripke frame if the engine adds world-indexed predicates, explicit negative membership or stratified negation, and integrity constraints.  
**[well-established]** Full reference-UFO semantics over arbitrary possible worlds and arbitrary identity criteria is not reducible to decidable OWL-RL plus ordinary Horn N3.

### Shared RDF 1.2 carrier

**[plausible]** A concrete encoding can use either named graphs or RDF 1.2 reifiers:

```trig
:w1 a ufo:PossibleWorld ; ufo:assertionGraph :w1g .
:w1 ufo:accessible :w2 .
:alice ufo:existsIn :w1, :w2 .

GRAPH :w1g {
  :alice rdf:type :Person .
}

:r1 rdf:reifies <<( :alice rdf:type :Person )>> ;
    ufo:trueIn :w1 .

:r2 rdf:reifies <<( :alice rdf:type :Person )>> ;
    ufo:falseIn :w2 .
```

**[well-established]** RDF 1.2 triple terms need not be asserted, and reifiers are deliberately suitable for describing situations in which propositions hold. [RDF 1.2 Concepts §1.5](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-triple-terms)

**[plausible]** The engine should compile either representation into:

```text
exists(x,w)       holds(x,T,w)       notHolds(x,T,w)
accessible(w,v)   subtype(T,U)       identityProvider(T,K)
```

**[plausible]** A finite-world profile must declare its world set and membership data complete; otherwise absence of `holds` cannot soundly mean `notHolds`.

### Exact extension needed

**[plausible]** The minimal extension to an OWL-RL+N3 engine is a finite-modal Datalog layer with:

- **[plausible]** quotation/reifier or named-graph compilation into `holds/notHolds/exists/accessibility`;
- **[plausible]** integrity-constraint heads such as `violation(...)`;
- **[plausible]** explicit negation, or stratified negation over a declared-complete finite world set;
- **[plausible]** witness checking for anti- and semi-rigidity;
- **[plausible]** range-restricted identity-criterion rules.

**[well-established]** OWL 2 RL reasoning is polynomial-time, while unrestricted N3 with existential rule heads does not inherit that bound; decidability requires a restricted Datalog or known decidable existential-rule fragment. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/), [Existential N3](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/identifier/S1471068425000055/type/journal_article)

**[plausible]** SHACL-SPARQL is the simplest implementation for validation; finite stratified Datalog/N3 is preferable if proof-producing inference is required. [SHACL 1.2 SPARQL](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl12-sparql/)

## Family verdict

**Partly.**

- **[well-established]** **Yes** as RDF 1.2 representation: all constructs, formulas, worlds and statements-about-statements can be carried without a reification barrier.
- **[plausible]** **Yes** as decidable executable validation over a finite, explicitly complete set of situations, using the extension above.
- **[well-established]** **No** as a semantics-preserving implementation of unrestricted reference-UFO modal quantification inside ordinary OWL-RL+N3.
- **[plausible]** Full arbitrary-world semantics requires a genuine quantified-modal or higher-order prover; that extension is implementable as a separate regime, but not generally decidable and not an OWL-RL materializer.

**[well-established]** Therefore the prior “RDF cannot express statements about statements” rationale is incorrect. **[well-established]** The remaining boundary is modal entailment and completeness over possible worlds—not RDF reification.## Verdict

[well-established] Every listed UFO-A moment can be represented as RDF 1.2 data, including statements about statements. [well-established] RDF 1.2 triple terms denote propositions without asserting them, while `rdf:reifies` introduces a separately describable reifier; N3 additionally quotes whole graphs/formulae and supports rules over them. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-triple-terms), [N3 specification](https://w3c.github.io/N3/spec/).

[well-established] The remaining limitation is not reification but inference strength: OWL-RL plus safe Horn-N3 can execute structural moment rules, whereas unrestricted UFO dependence and disposition semantics quantify modally over possible circumstances/worlds. [plausible] Those modal conditions can be operationalized decidably over an explicit finite world/event carrier; complete unrestricted first-order-modal reasoning is not decidable in general.

```n3
# Normalize manifestation direction.
{ ?d ufo:manifestsIn ?event. }
=>
{ ?event ufo:manifestationOf ?d. }.
```

[well-established] These are range-restricted Horn rules and terminate over a finite RDF carrier when built-ins do not invent unbounded new terms. [well-established] OWL 2 RL itself has polynomial-time standard reasoning problems and is designed for rule-engine implementation. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/#OWL_2_RL).

[plausible] Counterexample validation—“`x` exists in world `w`, but its required bearer does not”—needs a closed, complete world graph plus scoped negation-as-failure, SHACL, or stratified Datalog. [well-established] Under RDF’s open-world semantics, absence of `y ufo:existsIn w` is not evidence that `y` does not exist there.

[speculative] If the semantics must create previously unknown manifestation events or possible worlds, use a guarded or weakly-acyclic existential-rule/chase fragment. [well-established] Arbitrary existential rules or unrestricted N3 rules do not preserve the termination/decidability guarantee of OWL-RL plus safe Datalog.

## Corrections to the local analysis

[well-established] The absolute claim in [expressivity-analysis.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/arch/expressivity-analysis.md:148) that RDF/N3 has no encoding for operators over propositions is too strong: RDF 1.2 represents single propositions as triple terms, and N3 represents compound quoted formulae. [plausible] The defensible replacement is: “quoted content is statable, but its domain-specific modal/intensional consequences require explicit rules or another entailment regime.”

[well-established] The “missing” reification, inherence, relator and disposition structures listed in [nsm-ufo-bridge.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:437) are missing from the NSM profile, not inexpressible in RDF. [plausible] Its narrower distinction between executable OWL-RL and full reference-UFO modality remains valid.## Scope correction

- [well-established] RDF 1.1 reification, N3 formulae, and RDF 1.2 triple terms all permit statements about statements; therefore reification is not an expressivity wall.
- [well-established] RDF 1.2 triple terms denote propositions, may remain unasserted, and are normally connected to an ordinary resource through `rdf:reifies`; that resource can then be typed as a belief, claim, norm, commitment, etc. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-triple-terms).
- [well-established] RDF 1.2 is currently a Candidate Recommendation rather than a completed W3C Recommendation. [RDF 1.2 Semantics](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-semantics/).
- [well-established] N3 `{ … }` formulae provide quoted graph-valued content with variables and quantification. [W3C N3 submission](https://www.w3.org/TeamSubmission/n3/#Formulae).
- [well-established] Bare RDF supplies the carrier, not UFO’s temporal, intentional, causal, or deontic consequence relation; RDF explicitly anticipates separately defined semantic extensions and entailment regimes. [RDF 1.2 Semantics](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-semantics/#semantic-extensions-and-entailment-regimes).

Notation below uses `:r rdf:reifies <<( :s :p :o )>>`; the quoted triple is not asserted unless `:s :p :o` is also present.

## Family verdict

- [well-established] **UFO-B: yes at the representation level.** Events, participation, temporal parts, mereology, causation and situations all have direct RDF node/reifier encodings.
- [plausible] **UFO-B’s ordinary executable fragment fits finite safe Datalog over explicit events, intervals and worlds**, giving decidable materialization; unrestricted fusion axioms or counterfactual causation require existential/FOL/modal extensions with weaker decidability.
- [well-established] **UFO-C: yes at the representation level.** RDF 1.2 reifiers and N3 formulae remove the alleged inability to represent propositional attitudes, commitments, claims and norms.
- [plausible] **A practical UFO-C consequence relation can use function-free stratified Datalog over quoted content**, with explicit world/context/time parameters; it is decidable on finite datasets and implementable in existing rule engines.
- [well-established] **Bare RDF 1.2 alone does not validate the full intended UFO semantics.** It does not natively make beliefs opaque, evaluate goals, create obligations, enforce role anti-rigidity, or interpret named graphs as worlds.
- [plausible] **Full fidelity to arbitrary quantified modal/deontic UFO axioms requires quantified modal/deontic FOL or an equivalent theorem-prover layer.** Such a layer is implementable, but validity is undecidable in general, so timeouts and incomplete proof search must be exposed.
- [well-established] Consequently, the corrected answer is **“all listed constructs are statable; many useful fragments are decidably rule-executable; unrestricted full-UFO semantics is not guaranteed decidable.”**

The earlier axis-1 claims in [expressivity-analysis.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/arch/expressivity-analysis.md:26), especially G7/G10, should therefore be narrowed from “not statable” to “not natively interpreted by RDF/OWL entailment.”[VERDICT][SYN] The correction stands: statements-about-statements are available. The remaining gap is not reification but context-sensitive semantics—especially quantifying over situations, enforcing cross-situation identity, and validating existential/cardinality conditions. The smallest practical closure is a safe N3 profile over quoted situation graphs, plus a finite validation layer.

## Capability toolkit

| Toolkit | Expresses | Limits |
|---|---|---|
| RDF 1.2 / RDF-star | [STD] Triple terms can denote unasserted propositions; asserted triples remain graph members. Reifiers can carry provenance, confidence, temporal or modal metadata. Triple terms are transparent and currently occur as objects. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/), [RDF 1.2 Schema](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-schema/) | [STD][LIMIT] Quotation does not assert the quoted triple and supplies no implication, rule, possible-world, or situation semantics by itself. |
| RDF 1.1 reification | [STD] A resource with `rdf:subject`, `rdf:predicate`, and `rdf:object` describes a statement token, so statement metadata is representable. [RDF 1.1 Semantics](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-mt/#reification) | [STD][LIMIT] The reification neither entails nor is entailed by the base triple; identical components need not identify the same statement occurrence. Verbose and weakly axiomatized. |
| N3 | [CG] First-class quoted graph terms, variables, implication, graph/list/math/string built-ins, scoped graph inspection and scoped negation are available. [N3 Language](https://w3c.github.io/N3/spec/), [N3 Semantics](https://w3c.github.io/N3/reports/20230703/semantics.html) | [CG][LIMIT] It is a Community Group language, not a W3C Recommendation. Unrestricted built-ins, graph construction, network access and scoped negation do not give general termination or portable implementation behaviour. |
| OWL 2 DL | [STD] Decidable description-logic axioms: class constructors, disjointness, qualified cardinalities, property chains, inverses, keys and equality. [OWL 2 Syntax](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-syntax/) | [STD][LIMIT] No native possible-world/situation parameter. Metamodeling uses punning: the class and individual readings of one IRI are semantically separate. It cannot quantify over predicates or quoted assertions in DL. |
| OWL 2 RL | [STD] Horn-like, forward-rule-friendly subset supporting scalable type/property/equality closure. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/#OWL_2_RL) | [STD][LIMIT] Restricted cardinality and class-expression positions; it does not provide arbitrary existential witness generation, modal reasoning or semantically connected higher-order typing. |
| OWL 2 EL / QL | [STD] EL supports large existential-rich taxonomies with polynomial standard reasoning; QL supports ontology-mediated query rewriting over large relational ABoxes. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/) | [STD][LIMIT] EL omits most negation, universal and cardinality machinery; QL deliberately restricts constructors for first-order rewritability. Neither supplies UFO modal/metamodel semantics. |
| SHACL | [STD] Closed-data validation of types, paths, qualified counts, value ranges, equality/disjointness, logical combinations and closed shapes. [SHACL 1.2 Core](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl12-core/) | [STD][LIMIT] Conformance is about the supplied data graph, not truth in every model or possible situation. Missing data may be a violation under SHACL while remaining merely unknown under OWL’s open-world semantics. |
| SHACL-AF rules | [CG] Triple rules and SPARQL `CONSTRUCT` rules can monotonically add derived triples and can be conditioned by shapes. [SHACL-AF](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl-af/) | [CG][LIMIT] It is a W3C Note/advanced feature, not an ontology semantics. General termination is not guaranteed, and rules still need an explicit convention for situations or named graphs. |
| SPARQL 1.2 | [STD] Queries asserted and quoted triples, datasets and named graphs; property paths express regular reachability; aggregation supplies `COUNT`, grouping and numeric summaries. [SPARQL 1.2 Query](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql12-query/) | [STD][LIMIT] Query results are graph-relative. Paths are regular path matching, not general rules; aggregates count query solutions, not unnamed OWL-model individuals. RDF graph names are merely paired with graphs and need not denote them. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-dataset) |

## UFO hard cases

| UFO feature | Existing coverage | Remaining requirement |
|---|---|---|
| Modal rigidity | [SYN] RDF/N3 can represent situations and quoted contents. | [LIMIT] A rule must quantify across accessible situations and condition membership on continued existence. The UFO definition is explicitly modal. [UFO overview paper](https://www.nemo.inf.ufes.br/wp-content/uploads/ufo_unified_foundational_ontology_2021.pdf) |
| Types of types | [SYN] RDF can state that a type instantiates another type; N3 rules may range over those terms. | [LIMIT] OWL DL punning does not connect the two semantic levels; cross-level constraints require rules or OWL Full-style metamodeling. |
| Identity criteria | [SYN] `owl:hasKey`, inverse-functional properties and `owl:sameAs` approximate operational keys. | [LIMIT] UFO identity principles are type-governed persistence criteria, not merely database keys; situation-relative persistence and criterion inheritance need explicit axioms. |
| Counting/cardinality | [STD] OWL DL expresses model-theoretic cardinality; SHACL and SPARQL count explicit values. | [LIMIT] OWL-RL cannot cover arbitrary qualified counts, while closed-data counts are not open-world existential consequences. |
| N-ary relators | [SYN] Not representationally hard: make the relator an individual and connect each participant by mediation roles. | [LIMIT] The hard part is existential dependence, distinct participants and qualified multiplicities. UFO requires a relator to mediate at least two distinct individuals. [OntoUML mediation](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/relationships/mediation/index.html) |

## Minimal extension: UFO-SN3

[PROP] **UFO-SN3** is a range-restricted, situation-scoped N3 profile layered over OWL-RL:

- [PROP] `holds(W,G)` relates a first-class situation `W` to a quoted N3 graph `G`; unlike a raw named graph, this relation has declared semantic force.
- [PROP] The entailment stratum is function-free positive Horn: no fresh blank nodes, graph-generating built-ins, remote dereferencing, negation or aggregation.
- [PROP] Variables may range over individuals, types, properties and situations, thereby supporting higher-order typing without OWL-DL punning.
- [PROP] Identity rules are equality-generating dependencies implemented by finite union-find/congruence closure.
- [PROP] A separate finite validation stratum admits stratified `notIncludes` and bounded distinct-value counts, but only for situations or predicates explicitly declared closed.
- [PROP] Lower cardinalities and anti-rigidity are validation obligations rather than invented existential witnesses.

[PROP] Representative rules:

```text
# Rigidity across accessible situations
Rigid(T) ∧ Accessible(W,V) ∧ ExistsAt(X,V)
∧ holds(W,{ X rdf:type T })
→ holds(V,{ X rdf:type T }).

# Type-governed identity criterion
IdentityKey(T,P) ∧ holds(W,{
  X rdf:type T. Y rdf:type T.
  X P K.        Y P K.
})
→ Equal(X,Y).

# Relator projection
holds(W,{
  R rdf:type Marriage.
  R mediates X. R mediates Y.
}) ∧ Different(X,Y)
→ holds(W,{ X spouseOf Y }).
```

[PROP][LIMIT] Composite identity criteria compile to multi-property equality rules. Qualified cardinalities compile to bounded-distinctness constraints; minimum cardinality and anti-rigidity require an explicit witness or counter-situation in a closed validation scope.

## Verdict

[VERDICT][SYN] A small monotone ruleset alone is insufficient: it cannot detect missing existential witnesses or express anti-rigidity counter-situations. SHACL rules alone likewise lack modal context semantics, while raw named graphs lack a standardized denotation relation.

[VERDICT][PROP] UFO-SN3 is decidable and terminating for finite input under the stated restrictions: positive function-free Datalog reaches a finite fixpoint; equality only merges existing terms; bounded validation is finite model checking. For fixed rules, entailment remains polynomial in data size. Unrestricted N3 or unrestricted first-order modal satisfiability receives no such verdict.

[LOCAL] The fit with `sparq-reason` is unusually strong: it already exposes N3 and OWL-RL materializers and a range-restricted monotone RIF-Core front end ([lib.rs](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq/crates/sparq-reason/src/lib.rs:25>), [rif.rs](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq/crates/sparq-reason/src/rif.rs:1>)); formula-scope built-ins are tested directly ([n3_formula_scope.rs](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq/crates/sparq-reason/tests/n3_formula_scope.rs:1>)). The estate report confirms OWL-RL preprocessing and the SHACL/OWL verification gate ([sparq-estate.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/reports/sparq-estate.md:109)).

[LOCAL][LIMIT] The remaining implementation work is narrow but real: add a supported `holds(situation, quoted-graph)` index/head operation, equality-generating heads for identity criteria, and the closed validation stratum. The current RIF-Core layer deliberately forbids equality heads, negation and aggregation ([rif.rs](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/sparq/crates/sparq-reason/src/rif.rs:29>)).

**[well-established]** RDF 1.2, OWL 2 and ordinary N3 do not themselves assign possible-world semantics to named graphs or triple reifiers; RDF 1.2 explicitly leaves such dataset semantics to an extension. ### Shared RDF 1.2 carrier

**[plausible]** A concrete encoding can use either named graphs or RDF 1.2 reifiers:

```trig
:w1 a ufo:PossibleWorld ; ufo:assertionGraph :w1g .
:w1 ufo:accessible :w2 .
:alice ufo:existsIn :w1, :w2 .

GRAPH :w1g {
  :alice rdf:type :Person .
}

:r1 rdf:reifies <<( :alice rdf:type :Person )>> ;
    ufo:trueIn :w1 .

:r2 rdf:reifies <<( :alice rdf:type :Person )>> ;
    ufo:falseIn :w2 .
```

**[well-established]** RDF 1.2 triple terms need not be asserted, and reifiers are deliberately suitable for describing situations in which propositions hold. [RDF 1.2 Concepts §1.5](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-triple-terms)

**[plausible]** The engine should compile either representation into:

```text
exists(x,w)       holds(x,T,w)       notHolds(x,T,w)
accessible(w,v)   subtype(T,U)       identityProvider(T,K)
```

**[plausible]** A finite-world profile must declare its world set and membership data complete; otherwise absence of `holds` cannot soundly mean `notHolds`.

| **Rigidity** | **[plausible]** Add dataset-aware rule: `violationRigid(T,x,w) ← rigid(T), instanceSomewhere(x,T), exists(x,w), notHolds(x,T,w)`. With closed finite membership, `not holds` may replace explicit `notHolds`. | **[well-established]** Yes over a finite explicit frame. **[well-established]** Positive OWL-RL/Horn alone cannot derive a violation from absence. | **[well-established]** This implements “every instance is in T in every represented situation in which it exists”; it does not quantify over unrepresented possible worlds. [OntoUML Rigidity](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/theory/rigidity/) |
| **Anti-rigidity** | **[plausible]** For every `x` ever in `T`, require a reachable witness `w` with `exists(x,w)` and `notHolds(x,T,w)`. Detect missing witnesses using SHACL-SPARQL `FILTER NOT EXISTS` or stratified negation. | **[plausible]** Yes on finite data. **[well-established]** Not expressible as ordinary monotone Horn materialization without supplied witnesses, negation or existential model generation. | **[well-established]** One counterexample individual is insufficient: anti-rigidity requires possible cessation for every instance. |
| **Semi-rigidity** | **[plausible]** Derive `essentialFor(T,x)` when no accessible counterworld exists and `contingentFor(T,y)` when one does; require at least one witness of each kind. | **[plausible]** Yes with finite complete worlds and stratified negation. **[well-established]** Not derivable in positive OWL-RL alone. | **[well-established]** Semi-rigidity is not merely an annotation meaning “neither proved rigid nor proved anti-rigid”; it requires both essential and contingent instances. |

### Exact extension needed

**[plausible]** The minimal extension to an OWL-RL+N3 engine is a finite-modal Datalog layer with:

- **[plausible]** quotation/reifier or named-graph compilation into `holds/notHolds/exists/accessibility`;
- **[plausible]** integrity-constraint heads such as `violation(...)`;
- **[plausible]** explicit negation, or stratified negation over a declared-complete finite world set;
- **[plausible]** witness checking for anti- and semi-rigidity;
- **[plausible]** range-restricted identity-criterion rules.

**[well-established]** OWL 2 RL reasoning is polynomial-time, while unrestricted N3 with existential rule heads does not inherit that bound; decidability requires a restricted Datalog or known decidable existential-rule fragment. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/), [Existential N3](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/identifier/S1471068425000055/type/journal_article)

**[plausible]** SHACL-SPARQL is the simplest implementation for validation; finite stratified Datalog/N3 is preferable if proof-producing inference is required. [SHACL 1.2 SPARQL](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl12-sparql/)

## Family verdict

**Partly.**

- **[well-established]** **Yes** as RDF 1.2 representation: all constructs, formulas, worlds and statements-about-statements can be carried without a reification barrier.
- **[plausible]** **Yes** as decidable executable validation over a finite, explicitly complete set of situations, using the extension above.
- **[well-established]** **No** as a semantics-preserving implementation of unrestricted reference-UFO modal quantification inside ordinary OWL-RL+N3.
- **[plausible]** Full arbitrary-world semantics requires a genuine quantified-modal or higher-order prover; that extension is implementable as a separate regime, but not generally decidable and not an OWL-RL materializer.

**[well-established]** Therefore the prior “RDF cannot express statements about statements” rationale is incorrect. **[well-established]** The remaining boundary is modal entailment and completeness over possible worlds—not RDF reification.## Verdict

[well-established] Every listed UFO-A moment can be represented as RDF 1.2 data, including statements about statements. [well-established] RDF 1.2 triple terms denote propositions without asserting them, while `rdf:reifies` introduces a separately describable reifier; N3 additionally quotes whole graphs/formulae and supports rules over them. ## Minimal executable N3 extension

```n3
# Inherence entails existential dependence.
{ ?m ufo:inheresIn ?b. }
=>
{ ?m ufo:existentiallyDependsOn ?b. }.

# A dependent present in a represented world entails its bearer/dependent there.
{
  ?x ufo:existentiallyDependsOn ?y;
     ufo:existsIn ?w.
}
=>
{ ?y ufo:existsIn ?w. }.

# A relator depends on every entity it mediates.
{
  ?r a ufo:Relator;
     ufo:mediates ?x;
     ufo:existsIn ?w.
}
=>
{ ?x ufo:existsIn ?w. }.

# Trigger satisfaction activates a disposition.
{
  ?d a ufo:Disposition;
     ufo:triggerCondition ?condition.
  ?condition ufo:obtainsIn ?w.
}
=>
{ ?d ufo:activatedIn ?w. }.

# Normalize manifestation direction.
{ ?d ufo:manifestsIn ?event. }
=>
{ ?event ufo:manifestationOf ?d. }.
```

[well-established] These are range-restricted Horn rules and terminate over a finite RDF carrier when built-ins do not invent unbounded new terms. [well-established] OWL 2 RL itself has polynomial-time standard reasoning problems and is designed for rule-engine implementation. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/#OWL_2_RL).

[plausible] Counterexample validation—“`x` exists in world `w`, but its required bearer does not”—needs a closed, complete world graph plus scoped negation-as-failure, SHACL, or stratified Datalog. [well-established] Under RDF’s open-world semantics, absence of `y ufo:existsIn w` is not evidence that `y` does not exist there.

[speculative] If the semantics must create previously unknown manifestation events or possible worlds, use a guarded or weakly-acyclic existential-rule/chase fragment. [well-established] Arbitrary existential rules or unrestricted N3 rules do not preserve the termination/decidability guarantee of OWL-RL plus safe Datalog.

## Corrections to the local analysis

[well-established] The absolute claim in [expressivity-analysis.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/arch/expressivity-analysis.md:148) that RDF/N3 has no encoding for operators over propositions is too strong: RDF 1.2 represents single propositions as triple terms, and N3 represents compound quoted formulae. [plausible] The defensible replacement is: “quoted content is statable, but its domain-specific modal/intensional consequences require explicit rules or another entailment regime.”

[well-established] The “missing” reification, inherence, relator and disposition structures listed in [nsm-ufo-bridge.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:437) are missing from the NSM profile, not inexpressible in RDF. [plausible] Its narrower distinction between executable OWL-RL and full reference-UFO modality remains valid.## Scope correction

- [well-established] RDF 1.1 reification, N3 formulae, and RDF 1.2 triple terms all permit statements about statements; therefore reification is not an expressivity wall.
- [well-established] RDF 1.2 triple terms denote propositions, may remain unasserted, and are normally connected to an ordinary resource through `rdf:reifies`; that resource can then be typed as a belief, claim, norm, commitment, etc. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-triple-terms).
- [well-established] RDF 1.2 is currently a Candidate Recommendation rather than a completed W3C Recommendation. [RDF 1.2 Semantics](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-semantics/).
- [well-established] N3 `{ … }` formulae provide quoted graph-valued content with variables and quantification. [W3C N3 submission](https://www.w3.org/TeamSubmission/n3/#Formulae).
- [well-established] Bare RDF supplies the carrier, not UFO’s temporal, intentional, causal, or deontic consequence relation; RDF explicitly anticipates separately defined semantic extensions and entailment regimes. [RDF 1.2 Semantics](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-semantics/#semantic-extensions-and-entailment-regimes).

Notation below uses `:r rdf:reifies <<( :s :p :o )>>`; the quoted triple is not asserted unless `:s :p :o` is also present.

## Family verdict

- [well-established] **UFO-B: yes at the representation level.** Events, participation, temporal parts, mereology, causation and situations all have direct RDF node/reifier encodings.
- [plausible] **UFO-B’s ordinary executable fragment fits finite safe Datalog over explicit events, intervals and worlds**, giving decidable materialization; unrestricted fusion axioms or counterfactual causation require existential/FOL/modal extensions with weaker decidability.
- [well-established] **UFO-C: yes at the representation level.** RDF 1.2 reifiers and N3 formulae remove the alleged inability to represent propositional attitudes, commitments, claims and norms.
- [plausible] **A practical UFO-C consequence relation can use function-free stratified Datalog over quoted content**, with explicit world/context/time parameters; it is decidable on finite datasets and implementable in existing rule engines.
- [well-established] **Bare RDF 1.2 alone does not validate the full intended UFO semantics.** It does not natively make beliefs opaque, evaluate goals, create obligations, enforce role anti-rigidity, or interpret named graphs as worlds.
- [plausible] **Full fidelity to arbitrary quantified modal/deontic UFO axioms requires quantified modal/deontic FOL or an equivalent theorem-prover layer.** Such a layer is implementable, but validity is undecidable in general, so timeouts and incomplete proof search must be exposed.
- [well-established] Consequently, the corrected answer is **“all listed constructs are statable; many useful fragments are decidably rule-executable; unrestricted full-UFO semantics is not guaranteed decidable.”**

The earlier axis-1 claims in [expressivity-analysis.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/arch/expressivity-analysis.md:26), especially G7/G10, should therefore be narrowed from “not statable” to “not natively interpreted by RDF/OWL entailment.”[VERDICT][SYN] The correction stands: statements-about-statements are available. The remaining gap is not reification but context-sensitive semantics—especially quantifying over situations, enforcing cross-situation identity, and validating existential/cardinality conditions. The smallest practical closure is a safe N3 profile over quoted situation graphs, plus a finite validation layer.

## Capability toolkit

| Toolkit | Expresses | Limits |
|---|---|---|
| RDF 1.2 / RDF-star | [STD] Triple terms can denote unasserted propositions; asserted triples remain graph members. Reifiers can carry provenance, confidence, temporal or modal metadata. Triple terms are transparent and currently occur as objects. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/), [RDF 1.2 Schema](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-schema/) | [STD][LIMIT] Quotation does not assert the quoted triple and supplies no implication, rule, possible-world, or situation semantics by itself. |
| RDF 1.1 reification | [STD] A resource with `rdf:subject`, `rdf:predicate`, and `rdf:object` describes a statement token, so statement metadata is representable. [RDF 1.1 Semantics](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-mt/#reification) | [STD][LIMIT] The reification neither entails nor is entailed by the base triple; identical components need not identify the same statement occurrence. Verbose and weakly axiomatized. |
| N3 | [CG] First-class quoted graph terms, variables, implication, graph/list/math/string built-ins, scoped graph inspection and scoped negation are available. [N3 Language](https://w3c.github.io/N3/spec/), [N3 Semantics](https://w3c.github.io/N3/reports/20230703/semantics.html) | [CG][LIMIT] It is a Community Group language, not a W3C Recommendation. Unrestricted built-ins, graph construction, network access and scoped negation do not give general termination or portable implementation behaviour. |
| OWL 2 DL | [STD] Decidable description-logic axioms: class constructors, disjointness, qualified cardinalities, property chains, inverses, keys and equality. [OWL 2 Syntax](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-syntax/) | [STD][LIMIT] No native possible-world/situation parameter. Metamodeling uses punning: the class and individual readings of one IRI are semantically separate. It cannot quantify over predicates or quoted assertions in DL. |
| OWL 2 RL | [STD] Horn-like, forward-rule-friendly subset supporting scalable type/property/equality closure. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/#OWL_2_RL) | [STD][LIMIT] Restricted cardinality and class-expression positions; it does not provide arbitrary existential witness generation, modal reasoning or semantically connected higher-order typing. |
| OWL 2 EL / QL | [STD] EL supports large existential-rich taxonomies with polynomial standard reasoning; QL supports ontology-mediated query rewriting over large relational ABoxes. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/) | [STD][LIMIT] EL omits most negation, universal and cardinality machinery; QL deliberately restricts constructors for first-order rewritability. Neither supplies UFO modal/metamodel semantics. |
| SHACL | [STD] Closed-data validation of types, paths, qualified counts, value ranges, equality/disjointness, logical combinations and closed shapes. [SHACL 1.2 Core](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl12-core/) | [STD][LIMIT] Conformance is about the supplied data graph, not truth in every model or possible situation. Missing data may be a violation under SHACL while remaining merely unknown under OWL’s open-world semantics. |
| SHACL-AF rules | [CG] Triple rules and SPARQL `CONSTRUCT` rules can monotonically add derived triples and can be conditioned by shapes. [SHACL-AF](https://www.w3.org/TR/shacl-af/) | [CG][LIMIT] It is a W3C Note/advanced feature, not an ontology semantics. General termination is not guaranteed, and rules still need an explicit convention for situations or named graphs. |
| SPARQL 1.2 | [STD] Queries asserted and quoted triples, datasets and named graphs; property paths express regular reachability; aggregation supplies `COUNT`, grouping and numeric summaries. [SPARQL 1.2 Query](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql12-query/) | [STD][LIMIT] Query results are graph-relative. Paths are regular path matching, not general rules; aggregates count query solutions, not unnamed OWL-model individuals. RDF graph names are merely paired with graphs and need not denote them. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-dataset) |

## UFO hard cases

| UFO feature | Existing coverage | Remaining requirement |
|---|---|---|
| Modal rigidity | [SYN] RDF/N3 can represent situations and quoted contents. | [LIMIT] A rule must quantify across accessible situations and condition membership on continued existence. The UFO definition is explicitly modal. [UFO overview paper](https://www.nemo.inf.ufes.br/wp-content/uploads/ufo_unified_foundational_ontology_2021.pdf) |
| Types of types | [SYN] RDF can state that a type instantiates another type; N3 rules may range over those terms. | [LIMIT] OWL DL punning does not connect the two semantic levels; cross-level constraints require rules or OWL Full-style metamodeling. |
| Identity criteria | [SYN] `owl:hasKey`, inverse-functional properties and `owl:sameAs` approximate operational keys. | [LIMIT] UFO identity principles are type-governed persistence criteria, not merely database keys; situation-relative persistence and criterion inheritance need explicit axioms. |
| Counting/cardinality | [STD] OWL DL expresses model-theoretic cardinality; SHACL and SPARQL count explicit values. | [LIMIT] OWL-RL cannot cover arbitrary qualified counts, while closed-data counts are not open-world existential consequences. |
| N-ary relators | [SYN] Not representationally hard: make the relator an individual and connect each participant by mediation roles. | [LIMIT] The hard part is existential dependence, distinct participants and qualified multiplicities. UFO requires a relator to mediate at least two distinct individuals. [OntoUML mediation](https://ontouml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/relationships/mediation/index.html) |

## Minimal extension: UFO-SN3

[PROP] **UFO-SN3** is a range-restricted, situation-scoped N3 profile layered over OWL-RL:

- [PROP] `holds(W,G)` relates a first-class situation `W` to a quoted N3 graph `G`; unlike a raw named graph, this relation has declared semantic force.
- [PROP] The entailment stratum is function-free positive Horn: no fresh blank nodes, graph-generating built-ins, remote dereferencing, negation or aggregation.
- [PROP] Variables may range over individuals, types, properties and situations, thereby supporting higher-order typing without OWL-DL punning.
- [PROP] Identity rules are equality-generating dependencies implemented by finite union-find/congruence closure.
- [PROP] A separate finite validation stratum admits stratified `notIncludes` and bounded distinct-value counts, but only for situations or predicates explicitly declared closed.
- [PROP] Lower cardinalities and anti-rigidity are validation obligations rather than invented existential witnesses.

[PROP] Representative rules:

```text
# Rigidity across accessible situations
Rigid(T) ∧ Accessible(W,V) ∧ ExistsAt(X,V)
∧ holds(W,{ X rdf:type T })
→ holds(V,{ X rdf:type T }).

# Type-governed identity criterion
IdentityKey(T,P) ∧ holds(W,{
  X rdf:type T. Y rdf:type T.
  X P K.        Y P K.
})
→ Equal(X,Y).

# Relator projection
holds(W,{
  R rdf:type Marriage.
  R mediates X. R mediates Y.
}) ∧ Different(X,Y)
→ holds(W,{ X spouseOf Y }).
```

[PROP][LIMIT] Composite identity criteria compile to multi-property equality rules. Qualified cardinalities compile to bounded-distinctness constraints; minimum cardinality and anti-rigidity require an explicit witness or counter-situation in a closed validation scope.

## Verdict

[VERDICT][SYN] A small monotone ruleset alone is insufficient: it cannot detect missing existential witnesses or express anti-rigidity counter-situations. SHACL rules alone likewise lack modal context semantics, while raw named graphs lack a standardized denotation relation.

[VERDICT][PROP] UFO-SN3 is decidable and terminating for finite input under the stated restrictions: positive function-free Datalog reaches a finite fixpoint; equality only merges existing terms; bounded validation is finite model checking. For fixed rules, entailment remains polynomial in data size. Unrestricted N3 or unrestricted first-order modal satisfiability receives no such verdict.

EYE differential test gate for the N3 reasoner.
//!
//! Each case is an `input.n3` + an `*-answer.n3` (the expected entailed triples). We run our
//! forward-chaining closure on the input and assert it contains every triple in EYE's answer
//! (a superset, since EYE answers are often query-projected to the conclusion while we emit
//! the full closure). `socrates.*` is vendored verbatim from the EYE repo
//! (eyereasoner/eye `reasoning/socrates`); the rest follow EYE's documented builtin semantics
//! for features our v1 covers (forward `=>` rules + comparison/functional `math:` builtins).
//!
//! This is the parity gate the project grows: as backward chaining (`<=`), path syntax
//! (`!`/`^`), and more builtins land, drop the corresponding EYE `reasoning/<case>` files in
//! here and add a `check!`.
//!
//! EYE runs most cases as `eye <data+rules> --query <query.n3>`: the query file's
//! `{goal} => {goal}` rule both poses the goal (driving backward `<=` rules) and projects
//! the output. Our harness concatenates data + query into one document — same semantics
//! for these cases, since a query rule is just a forward rule.

use sparq_core::dict::Dict;
use sparq_reason::reason_n3;
use std::collections::HashSet;

/// The closure of an N3 document as a set of canonical (s, p, o) strings.
fn closure_strings(src: &str) -> HashSet<(String, String, String)> {
    let mut d = Dict::new();
    let triples = reason_n3(&mut d, src).expect("reasoning failed");
    triples
        .iter()
        .map(|[s, p, o]| (d.term(*s).to_string(), d.term(*p).to_string(), d.term(*o).to_string()))
        .collect()
}

/// Assert our closure of `input` contains every triple in EYE's `answer`.
fn check(name: &str, input: &str, answer: &str) {
    let closure = closure_strings(input);
    let expected = closure_strings(answer);
    for t in &expected {
        assert!(
            closure.contains(t),
            "[{name}] EYE answer triple not derived by sparq-reason: {t:?}\nderived closure: {closure:#?}"
        );
    }
}

    /// `subject predicateObjectList .` with `;` and `,`. A bare
    /// blankNodePropertyList (`[ :p :o ] .`) needs no predicateObjectList —
    /// its triples were already emitted while reading the subject; N3 also
    /// allows a bare subject (`:a .`, `{:b} .` — zero predicates).
    fn statement(&mut self, out: &mut Vec<[Term; 3]>) -> Result<(), String> {
        self.ws();
        let bracket_subject = self.peek() == Some(b'[');
        let subj = self.term(out)?;
        if self.strict
            && !matches!(subj, Term::Iri(_) | Term::Blank(_) | Term::List(_))
        {
            return Err(format!("invalid Turtle subject {subj:?}"));
        }
        self.ws();
        if matches!(self.peek(), Some(b'.') | Some(b'}') | None)
            && (if self.strict {
                bracket_subject
            } else {
                true // N3: zero-predicate statements are legal
            })
        {
            if !self.eat(b'.') && self.strict {
                return Err("statement must end with '.'".into());
            }
            return Ok(());
        }
        self.predicate_object_list(&subj, out)?;
        if !self.eat(b'.') && self.strict {
            return Err("statement must end with '.'".into());
        }
        Ok(())
    }

    fn predicate_object_list(&mut self, subj: &Term, out: &mut Vec<[Term; 3]>) -> Result<(), String> {
        loop {
            self.ws();
            let (pred, swapped) = self.verb(out)?;
            loop {
                let obj = self.term(out)?;
                if swapped {
                    out.push([obj, pred.clone(), subj.clone()]);
                } else {
                    out.push([subj.clone(), pred.clone(), obj]);
                }
                if !self.eat(b',') {
                    break;
                }
            }
            if !self.eat(b';') {
                break;
            }
            while self.eat(b';') {} // `;;` is legal (empty list items)
            self.ws();
            if matches!(self.peek(), Some(b'.') | Some(b']') | Some(b'}') | None) {
                break; // trailing `;`
            }
        }
        Ok(())
    }

    /// A predicate position: returns `(predicate, swapped)` — `swapped` is the
    /// classic N3 inverse-predicate syntax `is EXPR of` (object and subject
    /// trade places); `has EXPR` is the explicit forward form.
    fn verb(&mut self, _out: &mut Vec<[Term; 3]>) -> Result<(Term, bool), String> {
        self.ws();
        if !self.strict {
            if self.starts_with("=>") {
                self.i += 2;
                return Ok((Term::Iri(LOG_IMPLIES.into()), false));
            }
            if self.starts_with("<=") {
                self.i += 2;
                // `conclusion <= premise` — log:isImpliedBy; `parse` routes it into
                // the backward-rule set (goal-directed resolution, see module doc).
                return Ok((Term::Iri(LOG_IMPLIED_BY.into()), false));
            }
            // `<- expr` — inverted predicate (N3 2023): `:s <- :p :o` ≡ `:o :p :s`.
            // Disambiguate from an IRIREF `<-s>`: an IRI closes with '>' before
            // any whitespace.
            if self.starts_with("<-") && !self.iri_ahead() {
                self.i += 2;
                let pred = self.term(_out)?;
                return Ok((pred, true));
            }
            if self.peek() == Some(b'=') {
                self.i += 1;
                return Ok((Term::Iri(OWL_SAME_AS.into()), false));
            }
        }
        // `a` keyword (rdf:type) — only when a standalone token. Under
        // `@keywords` the bare form needs 'a' declared; `@a` always works.
        let a_allowed = match &self.keywords {
            Some(kws) => kws.contains("a"),
            None => true,
        };
        if a_allowed && self.starts_with("a") {
            let j = self.i + 1;
            if j >= self.s.len() || (self.s[j] as char).is_whitespace() || self.s[j] == b'<' {
                self.i += 1;
                return Ok((Term::Iri(RDF_TYPE.into()), false));
            }
        }
        if !self.strict && self.starts_with("@a") {
            let j = self.i + 2;
            if j >= self.s.len() || (self.s[j] as char).is_whitespace() {
                self.i += 2;
                return Ok((Term::Iri(RDF_TYPE.into()), false));
            }
        }
        // `is EXPR of` — inverse predicate; `has EXPR` — explicit forward.
        if !self.strict {
            if self.keyword("is") {
                let pred = self.term(_out)?;
                self.ws();
                if !self.keyword("of") {
                    return Err(format!("expected 'of' after 'is <pred>' at byte {}", self.i));
                }
                return Ok((pred, true));
            }
            if self.keyword("has") {
                return Ok((self.term(_out)?, false));
            }
        }
        let pred = self.term(_out)?; // N3 allows paths in predicate position
        if self.strict && !matches!(pred, Term::Iri(_)) {
            return Err(format!("invalid Turtle predicate {pred:?}"));
        }
        Ok((pred, false))
    }

    /// After `<`, does an IRIREF close (a '>' before any whitespace)?
    fn iri_ahead(&self) -> bool {
        let mut j = self.i + 1;
        while let Some(&c) = self.s.get(j) {
            match c {
                b'>' => return true,
                b'<' => return false, // a second '<' — not inside any IRIREF
                c if (c as char).is_whitespace() => return false,
                _ => j += 1,
            }
        }
        false
    }

    /// Consume `kw` when it stands as a whole token (followed by whitespace);
    /// under `@keywords`, the explicit `@kw` form is always a keyword.
    fn keyword(&mut self, kw: &str) -> bool {
        self.ws();
        let at = self.peek() == Some(b'@');
        let start = self.i + usize::from(at);
        let end = start + kw.len();
        if self.s[start..].starts_with(kw.as_bytes())
            && self.s.get(end).is_none_or(|c| {
                (*c as char).is_whitespace() || matches!(c, b'.' | b';' | b',' | b')' | b']' | b'}')
            })
        {
            self.i = end;
            true
        } else {
            false
        }
    }

    // ---- terms ---------------------------------------------------------------
    /// A term, including N3 path expressions: `node (! pred | ^ pred)*`. `A!P` is the object
    /// reached by `A P ?o`; `A^P` is the subject reaching `A` via `?s P A`. Each step
    /// introduces a fresh variable and emits the connecting triple into `out` (so paths in
    /// rule premises join correctly).
    fn term(&mut self, out: &mut Vec<[Term; 3]>) -> Result<Term, String> {
        let mut base = self.atom(out)?;
        if self.strict {
            return Ok(base); // paths are not Turtle
        }
        loop {
            self.ws();
            match self.peek() {
                Some(b'!') => {
                    self.i += 1;
                    let pred = self.atom(out)?;

                    base = next;

                    // surface): consume and ignore the datatype.
                    self.i += 2;
                    let _ = self.read_iri_or_prefixed()?;

                Some(b'^') => {
                    self.i += 1;
                    let pred = self.atom(out)?;
                    let next = self.fresh_pathvar();
                    out.push([next.clone(), pred, base]);
                    base = next;
                }
                _ => break,
            }
        }
        Ok(base)
    }

    fn fresh_pathvar(&mut self) -> Term {
        // Path steps mint EXISTENTIALS (cwm prints them as bnodes); inside a
        // rule premise the blank→variable rewrite turns them into join vars.
        self.pathvar += 1;
        Term::Blank(format!("__path{}", self.pathvar))
    }

    /// A single atomic term (no path operators).
    fn atom(&mut self, out: &mut Vec<[Term; 3]>) -> Result<Term, String> {
        self.ws();
        match self.peek() {
            Some(b'<') => {
                let iri = self.read_iriref()?;
                if let Some(q) = self.quantified(&iri) {
                    return Ok(q);
                }
                Ok(nil_to_list(Term::Iri(iri)))
            }
            Some(b'"') | Some(b'\'') => self.read_literal(),
            Some(b'?') => {
                if self.strict {
                    return Err("variables are not Turtle".into());
                }
                self.i += 1;
                let v = self.read_name();
                if self.peek() == Some(b'@') {
                    // `?D@de` (euler-era language cast on a variable): ignore.
                    self.i += 1;
                    let _ = self.read_name();
                }
                Ok(Term::Var(v))
            }
            Some(b'_') => {
                // _:label
                self.i += 1;
                self.eat(b':');
                Ok(Term::Blank(self.read_blank_label()))
            }
            Some(b'{') => {
                if self.strict {
                    return Err("formulae are not Turtle".into());
                }
                self.read_formula()
            }
            Some(b'(') => self.read_collection(out),
            Some(b'[') => self.read_bnode_propertylist(out),
            Some(c) if c.is_ascii_digit() || c == b'+' || c == b'-' || c == b'.' => self.read_number(),
            Some(b'@') if !self.strict => {
                // `@true` / `@false` (the explicit keyword forms under @keywords)
                if self.starts_with("@true") {
                    self.i += 5;
                    return Ok(Term::Lit("true".into(), XSD_BOOLEAN.into(), None));
                }
                if self.starts_with("@false") {
                    self.i += 6;
                    return Ok(Term::Lit("false".into(), XSD_BOOLEAN.into(), None));
                }
                Err(format!("unexpected '@' token at byte {}", self.i))
            }
            Some(_) => {
                // prefixed name, `true`/`false`, or `a`
                let bool_allowed = |kws: &Option<std::collections::HashSet<String>>, w: &str| {
                    match kws {
                        Some(set) => set.contains(w),
                        None => true,
                    }
                };
                if bool_allowed(&self.keywords, "true") && self.keyword("true") {
                    return Ok(Term::Lit("true".into(), XSD_BOOLEAN.into(), None));
                }
                if bool_allowed(&self.keywords, "false") && self.keyword("false") {
                    return Ok(Term::Lit("false".into(), XSD_BOOLEAN.into(), None));
                }
                match self.read_prefixed_name()? {
                    Term::Iri(i) => {
                        if let Some(q) = self.quantified(&i) {
                            return Ok(q);
                        }
                        Ok(nil_to_list(Term::Iri(i)))
                    }
                    other => Ok(other),
                }
            }
            None => Err("unexpected end of input".into()),
        }
    }

    fn read_iriref(&mut self) -> Result<String, String> {
        if !self.eat(b'<') {
            return Err(format!("expected IRI at byte {}", self.i));
        }
        let mut iri = String::new();
        loop {
            let Some(c) = self.peek() else { return Err("unterminated IRI".into()) };
            match c {
                b'>' => {
                    self.i += 1;
                    break;
                }
                b'\\' => {
                    // only \uXXXX / \UXXXXXXXX are legal inside an IRIREF —
                    // and the DECODED character is held to the same charset.
                    self.i += 1;
                    let ch = self.read_unicode_escape()?;
                    if (ch as u32) <= 0x20 || matches!(ch, '<' | '>' | '"' | '{' | '}' | '|' | '^' | '`') {
                        return Err(format!("escaped character {ch:?} is not allowed in an IRI"));
                    }
                    iri.push(ch);
                }
                // IRIREF excludes control chars, space and <>"{}|^`
                0x00..=0x20 | b'<' | b'"' | b'{' | b'}' | b'|' | b'^' | b'`' => {
                    return Err(format!("illegal character 0x{c:02x} in IRI"));
                }
                _ => {
                    let len = utf8_len(c);
                    iri.push_str(
                        std::str::from_utf8(&self.s[self.i..self.i + len])
                            .map_err(|e| e.to_string())?,
                    );
                    self.i += len;
                }
            }
        }
        Ok(resolve_iri(&self.base, &iri))
    }

    /// `\uXXXX` or `\UXXXXXXXX` with the backslash already consumed.
    fn read_unicode_escape(&mut self) -> Result<char, String> {
        let kind = self.peek().ok_or("truncated escape")?;
        let digits = match kind {
            b'u' => 4,
            b'U' => 8,
            other => return Err(format!("illegal escape '\\{}'", other as char)),
        };
        self.i += 1;
        if self.i + digits > self.s.len() {
            return Err("truncated unicode escape".into());
        }
        let hex = std::str::from_utf8(&self.s[self.i..self.i + digits]).map_err(|e| e.to_string())?;
        let cp = u32::from_str_radix(hex, 16).map_err(|_| format!("bad unicode escape '{hex}'"))?;
        self.i += digits;
        char::from_u32(cp).ok_or_else(|| format!("invalid code point U+{cp:X}"))
    }

    fn read_pname_prefix(&mut self) -> Result<String, String> {
        // read up to ':' (dots are legal inside a prefix name: `e.g:`)
        let start = self.i;
        while self.i < self.s.len() && self.s[self.i] != b':' && !(self.s[self.i] as char).is_whitespace() {
            self.i += 1;
        }
        let pfx = std::str::from_utf8(&self.s[start..self.i]).unwrap().to_string();
        if self.peek() != Some(b':') {
            return Err(format!("expected ':' after prefix name at byte {}", self.i));
        }
        self.i += 1;
        Ok(pfx)
    }

    fn read_prefixed_name(&mut self) -> Result<Term, String> {
        let start = self.i;
        let mut tok = String::new();
        while self.i < self.s.len() {
            let c = self.s[self.i];
            if (c as char).is_whitespace()
                || matches!(c, b';' | b',' | b']' | b'}' | b')' | b'(' | b'[' | b'{' | b'!' | b'^' | b'#')
            {
                break; // '#' starts a comment — never part of an (unescaped) pname
            }
            if c == b'\\' {
                // PN_LOCAL_ESC: the escaped punctuation char, literally.
                self.i += 1;
                let Some(e) = self.peek() else { return Err("truncated pname escape".into()) };
                if !br"_~.-!$&'()*+,;=/?#@%".contains(&e) {
                    return Err(format!("illegal pname escape '\\{}'", e as char));
                }
                tok.push(e as char);
                self.i += 1;
                continue;
            }
            if c == b'.' {
                // a dot is part of the name only when followed by another
                // name character (`:a.b` yes; `:a. ` no — statement dot).
                let cont = self.s.get(self.i + 1).is_some_and(|&n| {
                    (n as char).is_alphanumeric()
                        || matches!(n, b'_' | b'-' | b'.' | b'%' | b'\\' | b':')
                        || n >= 0x80
                });
                if !cont {
                    break;
                }
                if self.strict && tok.ends_with(':') {
                    return Err("local name cannot start with '.'".into());
                }
                tok.push('.');
                self.i += 1;
                continue;
            }
            if self.strict {
                // W3C Turtle PN rules on the RAW characters (escapes were
                // handled above): '%' needs two hex digits; other ASCII
                // punctuation must be escaped; locals cannot start with '-'.
                if c == b'%' {
                    let ok = self.s.get(self.i + 1).is_some_and(u8::is_ascii_hexdigit)
                        && self.s.get(self.i + 2).is_some_and(u8::is_ascii_hexdigit);
                    if !ok {
                        return Err("bad %-sequence in prefixed name".into());
                    }

                    return Err("local name cannot start with '-'".into());
                } else if c.is_ascii()
                    && !c.is_ascii_alphanumeric()

                None => return Err(format!("unknown prefix '{pfx}:'")),
            },
        };
        Ok(Term::Iri(format!("{ns}{local}")))
    }

    fn read_name(&mut self) -> String {
        let start = self.i;
        while self.i < self.s.len() {
            let c = self.s[self.i];
            if c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == b'_' || c == b'-' {

            } else if c >= 0x80 {

            } else {
                break;
            }
        }
        String::from_utf8_lossy(&self.s[start..self.i]).into_owned()
    }
